source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure User-Managed/External Keys for Service Accounts Are Rotated Every 90 Days or Fewer

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure User-Managed/External Keys for Service Accounts Are Rotated Every 90 Days or Fewer
input_row.description: Service Account keys consist of a key ID (Private_key_Id) and Private key, which are used to sign programmatic requests users make to Google cloud services accessible to that particular service account. It is recommended that all Service Account keys are regularly rotated.
input_row.rationale: Rotating Service Account keys will reduce the window of opportunity for an access key that is associated with a compromised or terminated account to be used. Service Account keys should be rotated to ensure that data cannot be accessed with an old key that might have been lost, cracked, or stolen. Each service account is associated with a key pair managed by Google Cloud Platform (GCP). It is used for service-to-service authentication within GCP. Google rotates the keys daily. GCP provides the option to create one or more user-managed (also called external key pairs) key pairs for use from outside GCP (for example, for use with Application Default Credentials). When a new key pair is created, the user is required to download the private key (which is not retained by Google). With external keys, users are responsible for keeping the private key secure and other management operations such as key rotation. External keys can be managed by the IAM API, gcloud command-line tool, or the Service Accounts page in the Google Cloud Platform Console. GCP facilitates up to 10 external service account keys per service account to facilitate key rotation. Impact: Rotating service account keys will break communication for dependent applications. Dependent applications need to be configured manually with the new key ID displayed in the Service account keys section and the private key downloaded by the user.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section Service Account Keys, for every External (user-managed) service account key listed ensure the creation date is within the past 90 days. From Google Cloud CLI 1. List all Service accounts from a project. gcloud iam service-accounts list 2. For every service account list service account keys. gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --iam-account [Service_Account_Email_Id] --format=json 3. Ensure every service account key for a service account has a "validAfterTime" value within the past 90 days.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console Delete any external (user-managed) Service Account Key older than 90 days: 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the Section Service Account Keys, for every external (user-managed) service account key where creation date is greater than or equal to the past 90 days, click Delete Bin Icon to Delete Service Account key Create a new external (user-managed) Service Account Key for a Service Account: 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. Click Create Credentials and Select Service Account Key. 3. Choose the service account in the drop-down list for which an External (user- managed) Service Account key needs to be created. 4. Select the desired key type format among JSON or P12. 5. Click Create. It will download the private key. Keep it safe. 6. Click Close if prompted. 7. The site will redirect to the APIs & Services\Credentials page. Make a note of the new ID displayed in the Service account keys section. Default Value: GCP does not provide an automation option for External (user-managed) Service key rotation. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-service- accounts#managing_service_account_keys 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/iam/service-accounts/keys/list 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts Additional Information: For user-managed Service Account key(s), key management is entirely the user's responsibility.
input_row.impact: Rotating service account keys will break communication for dependent applications. Dependent applications need to be configured manually with the new key ID displayed in the Service account keys section and the private key downloaded by the user.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-service- accounts#managing_service_account_keys 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/iam/service-accounts/keys/list 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts Additional Information: For user-managed Service Account key(s), key management is entirely the user's responsibility.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control focuses on ensuring that user-managed/external keys for service accounts are rotated every 90 days or fewer. The audit instructions provide a clear path to verify this using Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and the GCP CLI, indicating that the control can be fully verified via GCP APIs and CLI commands.

- ✓ The audit mentions Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and GCP CLI commands to list service account keys and check their creation dates.
- ✓ GCP APIs are available to query this property, specifically the `gcloud iam service-accounts keys list` command allows checking the age of each key.
- ✓ This is a standard GCP service property related to IAM, pertaining to access control, and can be checked through IAM API.
- ✓ The requirement is about verifying management of IAM keys, which is typically automated in CSPM tools.

Given the reliance on GCP CLI and clear guidance in the audit field, this control is well-suited for automation.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_service_account_key_rotation_period_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

The automated program would perform the following checks:

- Use `gcloud iam service-accounts list` to enumerate all service accounts within the project.
- For each service account, execute `gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --iam-account [Service_Account_Email_Id] --format=json` to retrieve details of user-managed/external service account keys.
- For each key, validate the `validAfterTime` field to ensure it is within the past 90 days.
- Pass Criteria: All service account keys have a `validAfterTime` within the past 90 days.
- Fail Criteria: Any service account key has a `validAfterTime` older than 90 days.
- Provide actionable feedback for non-compliant keys, suggesting rotation to ensure compliance with the 90-day requirement.

This approach leverages existing GCP tools to provide a robust automated check for service account key rotation, ensuring organizations maintain compliance with the CIS Benchmark.
step2_review:
---
### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. The control can be fully checked using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI, without requiring manual review.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud iam service-accounts keys list` command exposes all user-managed keys and their metadata, including creation dates.
- **GCP CLI Commands:**  
  The described approach accurately leverages the CLI to enumerate and inspect service account keys.
- **Other GCP Tools:**  
  While GCP Config (now part of Security Command Center) and third-party CSPM tools can also automate this, the CLI/API method is sufficient and standard.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 9/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  The process described is technically correct:
  - Enumerate service accounts: `gcloud iam service-accounts list`
  - List keys: `gcloud iam service-accounts keys list --iam-account ... --format=json`
  - Check `validAfterTime` (creation date) for each key
- **gcloud Methods/CLI Commands:**  
  The commands are accurate and standard.
- **Manual Steps:**  
  Not applicable; the approach is fully automated.
- **Program Name Convention:**  
  The program name `gcp_iam_service_account_key_rotation_period_enforced` is clear and descriptive, but could be slightly improved for specificity (see below).
- **Minor Technical Note:**  
  The control is specifically about **user-managed/external keys** (not Google-managed keys). The `gcloud` command lists both, but the `keyType` field should be filtered for `USER_MANAGED` keys only. This is a subtle but important distinction.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Key Type Filtering:**  
  The automation should explicitly filter for `USER_MANAGED` keys (`keyType: USER_MANAGED`). Google-managed keys (`keyType: SYSTEM_MANAGED`) are not subject to this control and should be excluded from the check.
- **API Alternative:**  
  The same check can be performed using the [IAM API](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/list) for integration with custom tooling.
- **Service Account Scope:**  
  The approach assumes project-level enumeration. For organizations with many projects, ensure the automation can iterate across all projects or folders as needed.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  For clarity and alignment with best practices, consider:  
  `gcp_iam_service_account_user_managed_key_rotation_period_enforced`  
  or  
  `gcp_iam_service_account_key_user_managed_rotation_90_days`
- **Additional Output:**  
  Recommend the automation output the specific service account and key IDs that are non-compliant, along with their creation dates, for actionable remediation.
- **Edge Cases:**  
  Consider handling service accounts with no user-managed keys gracefully (should pass).

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is technically sound, leverages the correct GCP APIs/CLI, and aligns with security best practices. Only minor improvements are needed for completeness and precision.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Keep as Automated, but**:  
  - Add explicit filtering for `USER_MANAGED` keys in the automation.
  - Update documentation to clarify that only user-managed/external keys are in scope.
  - Optionally, refine the program name for specificity as suggested above.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section             | Assessment         |
|---------------------|-------------------|
| Approach Validation | AGREE             |
| Technical Accuracy  | 9/10              |
| Improvements        | See above         |
| Confidence          | HIGH              |
| Recommendation      | Modify            |

---

**Reviewer Notes:**  
Excellent foundation. With minor adjustments for key type filtering and naming, this control can be robustly and accurately automated using GCP native tools.
